5 min read
Just now

--

When you build a backend for an app, you eventually run into a fundamental question: where and how does my code actually run? For most of computing history, the answer was simple — you rented or bought a server, kept it running around the clock, and your code lived on it. Today, there’s a second option that flips that model on its head: serverless.
This guide breaks down both approaches in plain language, so you can understand not just what they are, but when each one makes sense.
What Is a Traditional Server?
A traditional server is a machine — physical or virtual — that runs continuously, waiting to handle requests. Think of an AWS EC2 instance, a DigitalOcean droplet, or a machine sitting in a data center running Node.js, Django, or Rails.
With this model, you (or your ops team) are responsible for:

• Provisioning the right amount of compute capacity in advance

• Patching the operating system and managing security

• Configuring load balancers and redundancy for high availability

• Scaling manually — or building automation to scale — as traffic grows or shrinks
The defining trait is that the server is always on. Whether it’s handling a thousand requests a second or sitting completely idle at 3 a.m., it’s running, and you’re paying for it.
What Is Serverless?
Serverless doesn’t mean there are no servers — there absolutely are. It means you don’t manage them. Instead, you write code as small, event-triggered functions, and a cloud provider (AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, Azure Functions, Cloudflare Workers) runs that code only when something triggers it: an HTTP request, a file upload, a new database row, a scheduled job.
Serverless charges for actual compute time, typically in 100-millisecond increments, and there’s no cost for idle capacity because the function doesn’t exist between invocations. When there’s no traffic, nothing is running and nothing is billed. Provisioning is dynamic and precise — some services break their charges down into those same 100-millisecond increments, similar to a pay-as-you-go phone plan.
Head-to-Head: Key Differences
Infrastructure management. With traditional servers, you own the entire stack — OS, runtime, scaling, patching. With serverless, the cloud provider manages the security of the underlying infrastructure, OS, and runtime, while you remain responsible for your own application code, data-access permissions, and configuration.
Cost model. Traditional servers typically involve predictable, fixed costs — an EC2 instance costs a set amount per hour whether it’s busy or idle — which can lead to over-provisioning or under-provisioning. Serverless flips that: traditional hosting charges for provisioned capacity regardless of how many requests it actually handles, so idle time still costs you, whereas bursty or unpredictable workloads favor serverless because you avoid paying for unused capacity.
Scaling. Serverless architectures are inherently scalable — the cloud provider automatically scales infrastructure to handle changes in workload, running functions in parallel across multiple instances to absorb traffic spikes. Traditional servers can scale too, but it typically requires manual intervention or purpose-built autoscaling infrastructure that you configure yourself.
Reliability and availability. Achieving high availability on a traditional server requires architecting across multiple availability zones and configuring redundant systems and disaster recovery plans, which adds complexity and cost — whereas most serverless platforms are inherently fault-tolerant and highly available by default, automatically spinning up a new instance if one fails.
Performance. Serverless isn’t free of trade-offs here. Functions that haven’t run recently can suffer from “cold starts” — a short delay while the provider spins up a fresh execution environment. Traditional servers don’t have this problem since they’re already warm and running.
Execution limits. Serverless platforms restrict you to the runtime environments, memory limits, and execution-time caps the provider supports — AWS Lambda, for example, caps execution at 15 minutes — and debugging becomes more fragmented since logs are distributed across many short-lived invocations rather than living on one server you can log into directly. Long-running processes, then, are usually a poor fit for serverless.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

When Should You Choose Which?
Reach for serverless when:

• Your traffic is unpredictable or spiky (think: a Black Friday sale, a viral post, or an app with irregular usage)

• You’re processing discrete events — image resizing, notifications, webhooks, scheduled jobs

• You want to minimize operational overhead and move fast without hiring a dedicated ops team
Reach for traditional servers when:

• Your traffic is steady and predictable, so you can right-size capacity in advance

• You run long-lived processes, persistent connections (like WebSockets), or need fine-grained control over the runtime environment

• You’re working with a legacy application that isn’t easily broken into small, stateless functions
Neither model is objectively “better” — serverless architectures deliver an average cost reduction for typical web applications with variable traffic, but the decision isn’t just about cost; it’s about matching architecture to your specific requirements.
The Hybrid Reality
In practice, most real-world systems don’t pick a single lane. Many large cloud-native applications use a hybrid architecture instead of relying exclusively on either model — mission-critical services and long-running workloads run on dedicated infrastructure, while serverless functions handle burst traffic, file processing, notifications, scheduled jobs, and background tasks. A typical setup might keep a core API on traditional servers or containers, while offloading things like thumbnail generation, email sending, or webhook processing to serverless functions.
Final Thoughts
If you’re just starting out, don’t overthink this decision. Build with whatever gets you moving fastest — often a traditional server or a PaaS like Render or Railway — and reach for serverless functions when you hit a specific problem it solves well: unpredictable spikes, isolated event-driven tasks, or a desire to stop thinking about servers altogether.
Understanding both models isn’t just an academic exercise — it’s a practical skill that shapes how you design systems, estimate costs, and explain trade-offs to your team.
